title: Nasogastric Tube: Insertion, Placement Verification, and Decompression
subtitle: A patient needs a nasogastric tube for gastric decompression. The nurse inserts it safely, verifies placement correctly (not by auscultation), manages low intermittent suction, and prevents complications.

patient.name: Nasogastric Tube
patient.admitDate: A patient needs a nasogastric tube for gastric decompression. The nurse inserts, verifies, and manages it safely.

vitals.temp: Keep blue pigtail up; don't clamp

nursesNote: A patient with a bowel obstruction needs a nasogastric tube for gastric decompression. The nurse positions the patient in high Fowler's, measures the tube nose-to-earlobe-to-xiphoid, lubricates it with a water-soluble lubricant, and inserts it while the patient flexes the chin toward the chest and swallows sips of water. If the patient coughs, chokes, or becomes cyanotic, the nurse withdraws the tube and reassesses the airway. Placement is confirmed by X-ray before any use; a gastric aspirate pH of 5 or less supports gastric placement, and the external length is marked and checked each shift. Auscultation of an air bolus is NOT reliable. For a Salem sump, low intermittent suction is used and the blue air-vent pigtail is kept above stomach level and never clamped. The nurse keeps the head of bed elevated to reduce aspiration, provides oral and nasal care, irrigates with normal saline for patency, and monitors output, intake/output, and electrolytes (prolonged suction can cause hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis).
providerOrders: Insert the NG tube in high Fowler's; measure nose-earlobe-xiphoid
Confirm placement by X-ray before use; check pH and external length
Use low intermittent suction; keep the Salem sump air vent open and elevated
Keep the head of bed elevated; provide oral/nasal care and irrigate for patency
Monitor output, intake/output, and electrolytes during gastric suction

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about NG tubes?

opts.text: An NG tube can decompress the stomach in a bowel obstruction
An X-ray is the most reliable way to confirm placement before use
Measuring nose-to-earlobe-to-xiphoid estimates insertion length
Auscultating an air bolus reliably confirms correct placement
A gastric aspirate pH of 4 supports gastric placement
Coughing, choking, or cyanosis during insertion should be ignored

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What guides NG insertion and care?

opts.text: Position the patient in high Fowler's for insertion
Have the patient flex the chin toward the chest as the tube passes the throat
Keep the Salem sump blue air-vent pigtail above stomach level and unclamped
Continuous high suction is the standard for a Salem sump
Keep the head of bed elevated to reduce aspiration risk
Prolonged gastric suction can cause hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to insert an NG tube

opts.text: Place the patient in high Fowler's and explain the procedure
Measure the tube (nose-earlobe-xiphoid) and mark it
Lubricate and insert as the patient flexes the chin and swallows
Verify placement by X-ray/pH before use
Secure the tube and document the insertion length
Confirming placement by X-ray before first use
Checking gastric aspirate pH
Relying on an auscultated air bolus to confirm placement
Marking and checking the external tube length each shift
Clamping the Salem sump blue air vent to stop leaking
Setting low intermittent suction on a Salem sump
Keeping the head of bed flat during suction/feeding
Irrigating with normal saline to maintain patency

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: During insertion the patient coughs, gags, and turns cyanotic
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop advancing the tube immediately
Withdraw the tube from the airway
Assess airway, breathing, and oxygen saturation
Allow the patient to recover, then reattempt when stable
Document the event and response
Verifying NG placement with an X-ray before the first use
Using auscultation alone to confirm placement before feeding
Keeping the head of bed elevated during suction/feeding
Clamping the air-vent lumen of a Salem sump
Providing oral and nasal care and checking the nares
Setting continuous high suction on a sump tube
Monitoring intake/output and electrolytes during suction
Ignoring a change in the external tube length
